---
title: ""
pagetitle: "Quan (Kyle) Zhang — Health Economist"
description: "Quan (Kyle) Zhang — health economist and data scientist at the University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa. Causal inference, machine learning, population aging, and health equity."
---

::: {.name-block}
![](assets/profile.jpg){.profile-photo}

<div class="name-text">
<h1 class="site-name">Quan (Kyle) Zhang</h1>
<p class="site-role">Health Economist · Data Scientist</p>
<p class="site-affil">University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa<br>Economics · UHERO · John A. Burns School of Medicine</p>
<p class="site-contact"><a href="mailto:quanz@hawaii.edu">quanz@hawaii.edu</a> <span class="dot">·</span> <a href="https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=lWeyFo4AAAAJ">Google Scholar</a> <span class="dot">·</span> <a href="https://orcid.org/0009-0009-2409-309X">ORCID</a> <span class="dot">·</span> <a href="https://github.com/QuanZhangkyle">GitHub</a> <span class="dot">·</span> <a href="https://www.linkedin.com/in/quanzhangkyle/">LinkedIn</a> <span class="dot">·</span> <a href="https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Quan-Zhang-96">ResearchGate</a></p>
</div>
:::

::: {.bio}
I am a **health economist and data scientist** at the **University of Hawaiʻi at
Mānoa**, with graduate training in **Economics** (Quantitative Economics &
Econometrics) and a completed graduate concentration in **Quantitative Health
Sciences** at the **John A. Burns School of Medicine**. I combine **causal
inference and applied econometrics** with **machine learning, SQL, and large-scale
data engineering** to study population aging, frailty dynamics, health shocks and
labor supply, disaster health economics, and health equity — working with
longitudinal microdata such as CHARLS, HRS, and SHARE in R, Python, SQL, and Stata.

As a Graduate Research Assistant at **UHERO**, I contribute to the NIH- and
state-funded Maui Wildfire Exposure Study (MauiWES). I am also an invited
collaborator and peer reviewer for the **Global Burden of Disease Study 2023** at
the **Institute for Health Metrics and Evaluation (IHME), University of Washington**.
:::

## Research Interests

- Health economics and policy
- Causal inference and applied econometrics for health and social outcomes
- Population aging, frailty dynamics, and health and labor supply over the life course
- Disaster health economics and community resilience
- Health equity and quantitative health sciences

## Methods & Tools

::: {.skills}
**Causal inference & econometrics** — difference-in-differences (staggered adoption), instrumental variables, event-study and panel methods, regression discontinuity, and double/debiased machine learning.

**Machine learning & statistics** — causal forests and heterogeneous treatment effects, gradient boosting (XGBoost), LASSO / elastic net, survival analysis, and multiple imputation.

**Programming & data engineering** — R, Python, SQL, Stata, Git, and LaTeX; high-performance computing; and large-scale health and social microdata (CHARLS, HRS, SHARE, NHANES, IPUMS, GBD).
:::

## Education

- **PhD in Economics** (Quantitative Economics & Econometrics), University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa, 2023–present
- **Quantitative Health Sciences** — graduate concentration, John A. Burns School of Medicine, University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa, 2024–2026

## News

::: {.news-list}
::: {.news-item}
[2026]{.news-date} New paper in *Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS)*: "Do Negative Social Ties Accelerate Aging in Adults, or Does Aging Erode Social Ties?"
:::

::: {.news-item}
[2026]{.news-date} Selected for the **AWARD Network Summer Institute** (UCSF; NIA/NIH-funded national cohort).
:::

::: {.news-item}
[2026]{.news-date} Awarded the **Bernie Campbell Fellowship Fund Award**, University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa.
:::

::: {.news-item}
[Nov 2026]{.news-date} Presenting at the **GSA 2026 Annual Scientific Meeting** (NDIRA Caregiving Symposium), National Harbor, MD.
:::
:::

## Selected Publications

::: {.pub-list}
::: {.pub}
Zhang, Q. (2026). Do Negative Social Ties Accelerate Aging in Adults, or Does Aging Erode Social Ties? *Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS)*, 123(20), e2608036123.

[<i class="bi bi-box-arrow-up-right"></i> DOI](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2608036123){.pub-link} [<i class="bi bi-mortarboard-fill"></i> Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=lWeyFo4AAAAJ){.pub-link}
:::

::: {.pub}
Zhang, Q. (2025). Overcoming bias in a fall prevention program in resource-limited areas. *JAMA*.

[<i class="bi bi-mortarboard-fill"></i> Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=lWeyFo4AAAAJ){.pub-link}
:::

::: {.pub}
Zhang, Q. (2026). Keep REMS focused on safety, not as a barrier to generics. *Health Affairs*.

[<i class="bi bi-mortarboard-fill"></i> Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=lWeyFo4AAAAJ){.pub-link}
:::
:::

[See all publications →](publications.qmd){.more-link}
